sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: SPACE created gray platform at r8-12, c20-24. Player at r14-18, c14-18. Goal at r49-55, c43-49.
- Goal model: Navigate to goal at r49-55, c43-49.
- Action model: SPACE creates gray platforms. Player can move to gray platforms.
- Plan: Go UP to r8-12, c14-18, then RIGHT to gray platform at r8-12, c20-24.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Player is now at r8-12, c20-24 (on the gray platform). Looking at the surroundings:
r8: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBgggggBBBBBBB
r9: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBgggggBBRRRBB
r10: BBbbBbbBBBBBBBggBggBBRRRBB
r11: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBgggggBBRRRBB
r12: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBgggggBBBRBBB
The player is at c20-24 (bbbbb). To the right is the gray platform (ggggg) at c26-30, then black wall (BBBBBBB) at c31-37, then red path (RRR) at c38-40.
Wait, the player is at c20-24, which is the gray platform. The gray platform is at c20-24, and to the right is black wall at c25-30, then red path at c38-40.
Looking at the maze:
r14: BBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWW
At r14, there's a white area (WWWWW) at c2-6 (c20-24), then black wall (BBBBBBB) at c7-13 (c25-31), then white area (WWWWWWW) at c14-20 (c32-38), then B at c21 (c39), R at c22 (c40), B at c23 (c41).
Wait, I need to be more careful with the column mapping. Let me look at the actual columns.
The player is at c20-24. Looking at r8:
r8: BBbbbbbBBBBBBBgggggBBBBBBB
The player (bbbbb) is at columns 2-6 (c20-24). The gray platform (ggggg) is at columns 11-15 (c29-33). The black wall (BBBBBBB) is at columns 7-10 (c25-28) and 16-22 (c34-40).
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me just look at the relative positions.
The player is at r8-12, c20-24. To the right is the gray platform at c26-30 (approximately). The player can move RIGHT to c26-30.
But wait, the gray platform is at c20-24, and the player is already there. So the player is on the gray platform.
Looking at the maze, the gray platform is at r8-12, c20-24. To the right of the gray platform is a black wall at c25-30, then the red path at c38-40.
The player can't move RIGHT because the destination is a black wall.
Let me try DOWN from the gray platform. The destination is r14-18, c20-24, which is a white area (WWWWW). So DOWN might work.
Actually, looking at r14:
r14: BBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWW
At c20-24, r14 shows WWWWW. So the destination is white. Earlier, I found that DOWN works when the destination is black, not white. But maybe the gray platform changes this.
Let me try DOWN from the gray platform.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at r8-12, c20-24 (on gray platform). To the right is black wall, then red path. Below is white area at r14-18, c20-24.
Goal model: Navigate to goal at r49-55, c43-49.
Action model: SPACE creates gray platforms. Player on gray platform may have different movement rules.
Plan: Try DOWN from gray platform to see if it leads to white area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN from gray platform
result = action(['DOWN'])
print(f"After DOWN: done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'b' and node['pixels'] == 24:
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Player: r{min(rows)}-{max(rows)} c{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check surroundings
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
pr = [n for n in nodes if n['color']=='b' and n['pixels']==24][0]
pr_min, pr_max = min([p[0] for p in pr['boundary']]), max([p[0] for p in pr['boundary']])
pc_min, pc_max = min([p[1] for p in pr['boundary']]), max([p[1] for p in pr['boundary']])
print(f"\nAround player:")
for r in range(max(0,pr_min-2), min(64,pr_max+10)):
    print(f"r{r:2d}: {ascii_lines[r][pc_min-2:pc_max+20]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
